title: Adopted son held in slaying of elder
summary: Adopted son held in slaying of elder By KAREN AHO Daily News reporter A Mountain Village man, apparently angered by a scolding from his elderly adoptive father Monday, grabbed a large-caliber rifle and fatally shot his father in the home they shared, Alaska State Troopers said. Matt Peterson, 81, died instantly, troopers said. Mike Peterson, 30, stayed with his dead father, crying and summoning help from neighbors, after a friend who had been drinking with the pair wrestled the gun away and ran, troopers and neighbors said. Peterson waited outside with a neighbor before going willingly with village and tribal police officers to the public safety building. Troopers said they arrested him there on a charge of first-degree murder. He remained in the Yukon-Kuskokwim Correctional Facility on Tu…

title: Judge hits governor's daughter with $100 fine
summary: Judge hits governor's daughter with $100 fine The Associated Press JUNEAU - Gov. Tony Knowles' 19-year-old daughter has been fined $100 for underage drinking. Devon Knowles pleaded no contest Friday in Juneau District Court. Judge Peter Froehlich levied the smallest possible fine, calling it standard for college students who are first-time offenders. The charge carries a maximum fine of $300. "I hope you learned something from being here today," Froehlich told Devon Knowles, who was accompanied by her parents. "Alcohol is a dangerous drug." She and four of her friends were cited for drinking at the Governor's Mansion early on the morning of June 19 while the governor and his wife, Susan, were out of town. Alaska State Troopers and Juneau police came to the house after one of the friends ac…

title: Homer landowners shun hunters
summary: Homer landowners shun hunters By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - For the third year in a row, property owners in the hills north of Homer have paid $2,000 for full-page ads in the Homer News and Homer Tribune telling hunters to keep out. It's a lot of money but well worth the peace of mind, said Nancy Hillstrand, a driving force behind ads taken out by an informal group calling itself Skyline Lands. She described the ads as an insurance policy. "The amount of gunfire we used to hear up on the hill was pretty tremendous," she said. In bold letters, the ad says: "Skyline Drive land use notice. Have you asked permission?" It shows a couple of maps covering roughly 3,000 acres along Skyline Drive and Ohlson Mountain Road. A checkerboard of properties is blacked out with a mag…

title: Kenai radio station must find new site
summary: Kenai radio station must find new site By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - KDLL, the central Kenai Peninsula's public radio station, hustled for two years on a shoestring budget to set up its first studio. Now it has to start from scratch - in a hurry - if it wants to continue to go live with local news and music. The building that houses KDLL's fledgling studio recently changed hands, and the new owner, Vern Lofstedt of Kenai, wants to sell the property. Lofstedt told KDLL three weeks ago that it has to be out by the end of August, the station's general manager said. Stunned station managers say it's physically impossible to meet that kind of deadline. Delicate and highly regulated equipment like microwave transmitters can't be unplugged and carted across town without pla…

title: Study finds no aquatic invaders
summary: Study finds no aquatic invaders By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter After three years of searching Alaska waters for the dreaded non-indigenous plants, animals and organisms that are wreaking havoc in harbor ecosystems around the world, a group of scientists has good and bad news for Alaskans: It appears few if any have been transported to Alaska waters, but it also appears that Alaska's pristine waters are vulnerable. The salinity and water temperatures found here could sustain many of the non-indigenous species that have shown up in ports around the country, according to Anson "Tuck" Hines, a marine ecologist from the Smithsonian Environmental Research Center. Hines headed up a team of the country's top taxonomists in a three-year study to examine whether the oil-tanker ballast water…

title: Father's survival 'amazing'
summary: Father's survival 'amazing' Man rescued 2 days after capsizing kills 3 By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter Two people are dead and one is missing and presumed dead after a skiff carrying four Canadians on a family visit to an Alaska coastal village capsized in the Beaufort Sea, authorities said. The fourth person, the 36-year-old father of one of the victims, swam through stormy surf to a barrier island beach, where he awaited rescue for two days in what a North Slope Borough police officer called an "absolutely miraculous" survival. Dennis Arey of Aklavik, Northwest Territories, was staying Wednesday night with relatives in Kaktovik, a village on the north shore of Barter Island. He had been "big-time cold and almost incoherent" but was recovering, said borough police Sgt. Jonathan Owen. T…

title: Homeless vets get spit and polish
summary: Homeless vets get spit and polish Homeless veteran and StandDown participant Duane "Nanook" Marlette shines his boots Friday as he does every day. Marlette received his nickname several years ago when three women from the Northwest Territories opened his sleenking they were going to find a frozen body. When they found Marlette alive and well, one of the women said, "Oh, we have a Nanook here." Marlette has been in Alaska for six years. He served in Europe 20 years ago as an Army tank driver. Some of the free services available to the veterans at the StandDown were haircuts, medical and dental consultations, food, clothing, employment training, and chaplain, social service and financial couseling.
